notes.anchor: As to the shrine, if you find no gardens for me
notes.note: The horti are suburban pleasure-gardens across the Tiber from Rome; Cicero wants to buy a set as the site for Tullia's shrine. A frequented, public-facing location near the city was essential to his plan to make her memory permanent.

notes.anchor: But somehow I find myself wanting a frequented spot
notes.note: Cicero wants the shrine on a celebris locus, a much-visited site, so that crowds passing by will keep Tullia's cult alive; a remote tomb would be forgotten. This is why he prefers suburban gardens over his own country villas.

notes.anchor: The greatest throng is at Scapula's
notes.note: The horti Scapulani were the gardens of the late P. Scapula, now held by his coheirs; their position made them the most public and so most desirable site for Cicero's shrine. Much of the 45 BC correspondence turns on prying them loose at auction.

notes.anchor: I should very much like you to meet Otho, if he is in Rome
notes.note: Otho was a part-owner or rival bidder in the Scapula gardens whose involvement Cicero hoped to turn to advantage. His name recurs through these letters as the hunt for the shrine-site stalls and revives.

notes.anchor: For Drusus certainly wants to sell
notes.note: Drusus (M. Livius Drusus Claudianus) owned another set of gardens Cicero considered for the shrine and was, unlike the Scapula heirs, a willing seller. He is the contemporary property-owner of 45 BC, not the famous tribune of the same family.
